1: The third year of Belshazzar (ca. 551 BCE): Daniel receives a second personal vision. The date places this vision two years before the first vision (Daniel 7: first year of Belshazzar) — the chapters are not in strict chronological order. Chapter 8 transitions from Aramaic back to Hebrew (the Aramaic section ends at Daniel 7:28) — the shift in language has been variously explained as relating to the audience (Aramaic for universal/Gentile content, Hebrew for Israel-specific content) or to the documentary sources of the book. Chapter 8 is the most historically precise of Daniel's visions, with the ram and goat being explicitly identified as Persia and Greece by the angel Gabriel.
2: Susa (Shushan) in the province of Elam: Susa was the main Achaemenid royal residence (winter capital), not Babylon. The Ulai canal (ha-ʿUvil) was a waterway near Susa confirmed by Persian-period inscriptions. The detail is geographically precise — not a generic vision-landscape but a specific Persian administrative capital. This precision is one of the markers that conservative scholars point to as evidence of authentic historical memory: the setting has the specificity of eyewitness geography, not the vagueness of pseudonymous composition two centuries later.
3: The ram with two horns (ayil ʾekhad qodam hayʾor u-lo sheta qarnayim): standing before the canal, with two horns, one higher than the other with the higher coming up last. Gabriel's interpretation (Daniel 8:20) explicitly identifies this as the kings of Media and Persia. The two horns of different heights correspond to the two components of the dual empire — Media was the senior partner historically but Persia became dominant. The Cyrus Cylinder (written from Cyrus's perspective) emphasizes the Median-Persian alliance, with Cyrus ruling as “king of Persia and the Medes.”
4: The ram charging westward, northward, and southward — and no beast can withstand it or be rescued from its power. The three directions of Persian expansion: westward (toward Greece, Lydia, and the Aegean), northward (toward Scythia, the Black Sea), southward (toward Egypt and Ethiopia). The exception: eastward is not mentioned — Persia's origin was in the east and it was never seriously challenged from that direction until Alexander. The verse captures the Persian empire's near-universal military dominance during its peak (6th-5th centuries BCE).
5: The male goat from the west (tzafir ha-ʿizzim) coming across the face of the whole earth without touching the ground: Gabriel's interpretation (Daniel 8:21) identifies this as the king of Greece, the great horn as the first king. Alexander the Great's speed of conquest — “without touching the ground” — is not mere hyperbole but historically accurate. His army covered approximately 21,000 miles in 13 years, moving at an average of roughly 20 miles per day in campaign mode, an unprecedented rate of territorial expansion in the ancient world.
6: The goat charges the ram with great rage (chemah uzzo, fury and strength): this is Alexander's campaign against Persia (334-331 BCE). The confrontation at the Granicus (334), Issus (333), and Gaugamela (331) represents the three major battles in which Alexander decisively defeated Darius III. The phrase “came to the ram that had the two horns” describes the deliberate westward turn of Alexander's campaign toward Persia specifically — he bypassed other targets to reach the Persian heartland.
7: The goat strikes the ram and breaks both its horns: the Seleucid Babylonian Chronicle records that Darius III fled after Gaugamela and was murdered by his own satrap Bessus in 330 BCE, ending Achaemenid rule. No one could rescue the ram from the goat — consistent with the historical reality that no power successfully resisted Alexander's advance once he crossed the Hellespont. The thoroughness of Alexander's victory and the speed of Persian empire's collapse were recognized as extraordinary even by contemporaries.
8: The great horn broken at the height of its power: Alexander died in Babylon in June 323 BCE at age 32 — at the absolute height of his power, with no military defeat, only days after completing his last campaign. The cause of death is disputed (fever, possibly typhoid, possibly alcohol-related illness). Four notable horns replace the great one: this corresponds precisely to the Wars of the Diadochi (Successor Wars, 323-281 BCE) that resulted in four major successor kingdoms: Ptolemaic Egypt, Seleucid Syria/East, Antigonid Macedonia, and Attalid Asia Minor.
9: Out of one of the four horns a small horn grows exceedingly great toward the south, east, and the glorious land (Eretz ha-Tzvi, the beautiful/glorious land — a standard epithet for Israel). This is Antiochus IV Epiphanes (175-164 BCE), who arose from the Seleucid line (one of the four Diadoch kingdoms). His campaigns: southward against Egypt (two invasions, 170 and 168 BCE), eastward against Persia and Parthia, and toward the “glorious land” — Israel, which he conquered and persecuted. The identification is confirmed by Gabriel in Daniel 8:23-25.
10: The horn grows to the host of heaven and casts down some of the host and stars to the ground: the language shifts into cosmic symbolism. The “host of heaven” (tzva ha-shamayim) can refer to (1) the Jewish people as YHWH's heavenly army on earth, (2) the angelic host, or (3) the Zadokite priesthood as the divinely appointed administration of the temple. Antiochus IV deposed and murdered legitimate high priests (Jason and Onias III), installed Hellenized priests, and killed thousands of Torah-observant Jews — literal casting down of the people of the host of heaven.
11: The horn magnifies itself to the Prince of the host and takes away the regular burnt offering (tamid) and throws down the place of his sanctuary. Historically: Antiochus IV abolished the tamid (daily morning and evening burnt offering, Exodus 29:38-42) in December 167 BCE, erected an altar to Zeus Olympios in the temple courts, and sacrificed a pig on it — the “abomination of desolation” (1 Macc Daniel 1:54; Daniel 11:31; Matthew 24:15). The “Prince of the host” (sar ha-tzva) may be the high priest or YHWH himself — probably YHWH, since the horn magnifies itself against the divine.
12: A host given over to the horn in transgression (v-tzava tinaten ʿal ha-tamid b-pesha): the abolition of the daily offering represents the complete disruption of Israel's covenant worship. The phrase v-tashlich emet artzah (it threw truth to the ground) describes the abolition of Torah: Antiochus IV specifically ordered the burning of Torah scrolls and forbade Torah study under pain of death (1 Macc Daniel 1:56-57). “It acted and prospered” — Antiochus IV's initial success in suppressing Jewish observance is acknowledged as real: for approximately three years, the abomination held.
13: The holy one's question (lemata ha-chazon ha-tamid v-ha-pesha shomem): for how long will the vision of the regular burnt offering and the desolating transgression be? The term shomem (causing desolation/horror) is the root of the shiqquts meshomem (abomination of desolation) in Daniel 11:31 and Matthew 24:15. The question reflects the community of worshippers' deepest concern: how long until YHWH restores the worship of the temple? The same question echoes in Psalms 74:9-10 (“we do not see our signs ... how long, O God, is the foe to scoff?”) and in Revelation 6:10 (“how long?”).
14: The answer: 2,300 evenings and mornings (erev boker, evening-morning), then the sanctuary shall be restored to its rightful state (v-nitzdaq qodesh). The 2,300 evening-morning pairs have been interpreted as: (1) 2,300 days (just over six years); (2) 1,150 days (about three years and two months), counting each sacrifice separately (morning and evening = two daily occurrences). The Maccabean restoration of the temple on the 25th of Kislev 164 BCE was exactly three years after Antiochus's desecration on the 25th of Kislev 167 BCE — approximately 1,095-1,100 days. Hanukkah commemorates this restoration.
15: Gabriel appears for the first time in Scripture here — the only angel in the OT explicitly named as Gabriel (the other is Michael; cf. Daniel 9:21; Daniel 10:13, 21). Gabriel (Hebrew gavri-el, man of God or strong man of God) appears in subsequent Jewish tradition in 1 Enoch Daniel 9:1; 20:7 and the NT at Luke 1:19, 26 as the angel of annunciation to Zechariah and Mary. His first OT appearance is as the interpreter of Daniel's vision — his role in Luke 1 as announcer of the eschatological fulfillment is continuous with his Daniel role: the same angel interprets the same eschatological program at different stages of its unfolding.
16: A voice from the Ulai canal commands Gabriel: Gavriel haveen et-hamarah hallaz (Gabriel, make this one understand the vision). The voice from the water-channel commands the named angel — this is the divine council at work: an unidentified superior voice (YHWH?) directs Gabriel. The structure is the same as Zechariah's night visions (Zechariah 1:9ff: an interpreting angel who himself receives commands from a superior). The layered hierarchy of the heavenly court — YHWH → senior angels → interpreting angels → human prophets — is one of Daniel's contributions to Second Temple angelology.
17: Gabriel approaches Daniel and Daniel falls prostrate in terror (vatipol ʿal-panai): the standard response to angelophany in the OT (Judges 13:20; Isaiah 6:5; Ezekiel 1:28; Revelation 1:17). Gabriel's identification of Daniel as ben-adam (son of man) — the human being — is ironic given the immediately preceding vision of the Son of Man who receives divine dominion (Daniel 7). Here ben-adam means “mere mortal” in contrast to the heavenly being. The phrase ki le-ʿet qetz ha-chazon (for the vision is for the time of the end) establishes that the vision's fulfillment is future — specifically future relative to the vision's date (551 BCE).
18: Daniel falls into a deep sleep (nirdam ʿal-panai artzah, fell into a deep sleep with my face to the ground) — the same trance-state as Abraham in Genesis 15:12 (tardemah, deep sleep). The deep sleep at the moment of divine communication is the ANE and OT pattern for the receptive state of prophetic experience. Gabriel touches him and makes him stand: the physical touch that restores the prophet from trance to active engagement appears also in Ezekiel (Ezekiel 2:1-2) and in John's Patmos vision (Revelation 1:17). The act of being raised from the ground by the angel prefigures the resurrection language of later apocalyptic.
19: Gabriel restates the interpretive framework: hineni modiaʿakha et asher-yihyeh b'acharit ha-zaʿam ki l'moʿed qetz (I will make known to you what shall be at the end of the indignation, for it refers to the appointed time of the end). The zaʿam (indignation/wrath) is YHWH's wrath against his people expressed through the persecution — a standard OT concept (Isaiah 10:5, 25; Daniel 26:20). The moʿed qetz (appointed time of the end) uses the technical term moʿed (appointed time/feast) — even the time of tribulation is YHWH's appointed meeting with history.
20: The explicit identification of the ram: ha-ayil asher rʾita baʿal ha-qarnayim malkhei Madai u-Faras (the ram with the two horns represents the kings of Media and Persia). Gabriel's interpretation makes Daniel 8 the most explicitly interpreted vision in the book — unlike Daniel 7 where the identification of the four kingdoms is deduced, here Gabriel names the first two empires directly. The dual empire corresponds to the historical Achaemenid kingdom's dual Median-Persian identity — Cyrus ruled both peoples and presented himself as king of both nations on the Cyrus Cylinder.
21: The male goat is the king of Greece (Yavan, Iavan — the standard Hebrew name for Greece/Ionians): ha-tzafir ha-saʿir melekh Yavan veha-qeren ha-gedolah asher bein ʿeinav hu ha-melekh ha-rishon (the male goat is the king of Greece and the great horn between his eyes is the first king). “The first king” is Alexander the Great — the only plausible identification given the historical fit (the great horn that is broken at the height of power, replaced by four, with a little horn arising from one of the four). The precision of the identification suggests either genuine prediction or that the author was writing from the Maccabean period — the two interpretive options that have divided scholarship.
22: The four horns replacing the broken great horn: v'ha-ʿarbaʿ asher qamu tachtav arbaʿ malkhuyot mi-goy yaʿamodu v'lo b'khocho (and the four that stood in his place, four kingdoms will arise from his nation, but not with his power). The qualification “not with his power” is historically exact: none of Alexander's successors achieved his scope of conquest or his unifying vision. The Diadochi spent 40 years fighting each other (Wars of the Successors, 323-281 BCE) and the resulting kingdoms were always weaker than Alexander's unified empire had been.
23: The king of bold face (melekh ʿaz-panim, king of a hard/fierce countenance) who arises at the end of their rule is Antiochus IV Epiphanes. The phrase uvihashlem ha-poshʿim (when the transgressors have reached their full measure) reflects a theme from Genesis 15:16 (the iniquity of the Amorites not yet complete) — YHWH permits wickedness to run its course until its “full measure” before judgment falls. Antiochus IV's reign represents the culmination of Seleucid Hellenizing pressure on Judaism — the extreme limit of the Greek empire's assault on Israelite covenant life.
24: Antiochus IV's power shall be great but “not by his own power” (v'lo b'khocho): interpreted as either (1) by cunning and alliance rather than military superiority (he took power by political manipulation, not conquest), or (2) his power comes from a demonic source. He shall destroy the mighty men and the holy people: historically confirmed — Antiochus IV's campaigns killed tens of thousands of Jews (2 Macc Daniel 5:11-14 describes 80,000 killed at Jerusalem) and destroyed the political power of the Zadokite priesthood.
25: By his cunning (v'b'sekhlo v'hitsliach miramah b'yado, by his cunning he will cause deceit to prosper) and he shall magnify himself in his heart and destroy many while they are at ease. The description of Antiochus IV's political methods matches the historical record: he gained power by political manipulation (bribery, presenting himself as a reformer), conducted surprise attacks (“destroy many while they are at ease” — the Sabbath massacre of 2 Macc Daniel 6:11), and ultimately challenged YHWH himself. U-vaʿamad ʿal-sar sarim (he will stand against the Prince of princes) — probably the high priest Onias III whom he had murdered, or YHWH himself. U-v'efes yad yishaver (he shall be broken — but not by human hand): Antiochus IV died of disease in 164 BCE.
26: The vision of the evenings and mornings is true (emet) — its truthfulness is asserted before sealing. The command to seal the vision (stom ha-chazon): sealing a prophetic document means preserving it for a future time when it will be understood and relevant (cf. Isaiah 8:16; Daniel 12:4). The phrase ki l'yamim rabbim (for it refers to many days from now) establishes that from Daniel's perspective, the vision's fulfillment is distant future. Daniel's position in the 6th century BCE means the events of 167-164 BCE are approximately 380 years away — “many days” indeed.
27: Daniel's physical collapse and illness after the vision: v'ni Daniel nikheiti v'chalitihi yamim (I, Daniel, was overcome and lay ill for some days). The psychosomatic impact of apocalyptic vision — being “appalled at the vision” (v'eshtemam ʿal ha-marʾeh) — is consistent with other prophetic experiences of encountering the divine (cf. Isaiah 21:4; Jeremiah 4:19; Ezekiel 3:15; Revelation 1:17). The vision exceeds ordinary human capacity to receive without physical consequence. The final note — he rose and did the king's business — shows Daniel returning to normal life, carrying the apocalyptic vision as hidden knowledge alongside ordinary royal administration.
